title: Preterite vs Imperfect (Indefinido vs Imperfecto)
description: The key choice in Spanish past tense: the preterite for completed, single events ('what happened') versus the imperfect for background, description and habits ('what was going on / used to'). Master the classic scene-and-interruption combo and the verbs that change meaning (conocí vs conocía, supe vs sabía, quise vs quería).

cards.notes: **Preterite** = a completed action, viewed as a finished whole: *Comí* = I ate (and finished). **Imperfect** = an action in progress, repeated, or habitual in the past: *Comía* = I was eating / I used to eat. English past simple ('I ate') can map to **either** — the meaning decides.

cards.front: hablaba, hablabas, hablaba, hablábamos, hablabais, hablaban
cards.notes: Regular **-AR** imperfect endings: **-aba, -abas, -aba, -ábamos, -abais, -aban**. Note *yo* and *él/ella* are identical (*hablaba*) — context or a pronoun tells them apart. The imperfect describes what *used to* or *was* happening.

cards.front: comía, comías, comía, comíamos, comíais, comían
cards.notes: **-ER** and **-IR** verbs share one set of imperfect endings: **-ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían**. Every form has a written accent on the *í*. So *comer* and *vivir* pattern alike: *comía / vivía*.

cards.back: the only 3 irregular imperfects: ser, ir, ver
cards.notes: Just **three** verbs are irregular in the imperfect: *ser* → **era, eras, era, éramos, erais, eran**; *ir* → **iba, ibas, iba, íbamos, ibais, iban**; *ver* → **veía, veías...**. *Era alto* = He was tall; *Iba al colegio* = I used to go to school.

cards.front: Hacía sol y ella era alta.
cards.notes: Use the **imperfect** to set the scene: weather, time, age, physical and emotional states, and ongoing background. *Hacía sol* = It was sunny; *Era alta* = She was tall; *Tenía diez años* = I was ten; *Estaba cansado* = I was tired.

cards.back: preterite for a chain of completed events
cards.notes: When you narrate a **sequence** of finished actions ('and then... and then...'), each one is in the **preterite**: *Llegué, comí y me fui* = I arrived, ate, and left. This is the storyline / 'what happened next' tense.

The classic combo: the **imperfect** sets an ongoing background (*dormía* = I was sleeping) and the **preterite** marks the event that interrupts it (*sonó* = rang). *While I was sleeping, the phone rang.* The longer 'was -ing' action is imperfect; the sudden one is preterite.

cards.back: verbs that change meaning by tense (1)
cards.notes: Some verbs shift meaning. **conocer**: *conocí* = I met (first time) vs *conocía* = I knew / was acquainted with. **saber**: *supe* = I found out / learned vs *sabía* = I knew (a fact). *La conocí en 2010* = I met her in 2010; *Ya la conocía* = I already knew her.

cards.back: verbs that change meaning by tense (2)
cards.notes: **querer**: *quise* = I tried / decided to vs *quería* = I wanted; *no quise* = I refused vs *no quería* = I didn't want. **poder**: *pude* = I managed to / succeeded vs *podía* = I was able / could. *Quise abrir la puerta* = I tried to open the door; *Pude abrirla* = I managed to open it.

cards.front: ayer, de repente, un día / siempre, mientras, todos los días
cards.back: trigger words that hint at each tense
cards.notes: **Preterite** cues (a finished moment): *ayer* (yesterday), *de repente* (suddenly), *un día* (one day), *dos veces* (twice), *anoche*. **Imperfect** cues (ongoing/habitual): *siempre* (always), *mientras* (while), *todos los días* (every day), *mientras tanto* (meanwhile), *normalmente*. Cues guide you, but meaning is the final judge.

cards.front: I was eating = comía  ·  I ate = comí / comía
cards.back: how English past maps onto the two tenses
cards.notes: English 'was/were + -ing' (*I was eating*) is almost always the **imperfect** (*comía*). 'used to' (*I used to eat*) is also imperfect. But the plain past simple (*I ate*) can be **either** — *Comí a las dos* (a single time) vs *Comía a las dos* (every day). Decide by asking: finished event, or ongoing/habitual background?

steps.title: Two ways to look at the past
steps.body:
# Preterite vs Imperfect

Spanish has **two** simple past tenses, and choosing between them is the single most important skill at A2. The good news: it is about **meaning**, not difficulty.

- **Preterite (indefinido)** — a completed action, viewed as a finished whole. Answers *what happened?*
  - *Ayer **comí** paella.* = Yesterday I ate paella.
  - ***Llegó**, **abrió** la puerta y **entró**.* = He arrived, opened the door, and went in. (a chain of events)

- **Imperfect (imperfecto)** — background, description, habits, or actions in progress. Answers *what was going on? / what used to happen?*
  - *De niño **comía** mucha pasta.* = As a child I used to eat a lot of pasta.
  - ***Hacía** sol y los pájaros **cantaban**.* = It was sunny and the birds were singing.

> The trap for English speakers: English past simple ('I ate', 'I lived') maps to **both** tenses. You cannot translate word-for-word — you must read the meaning first.

**Imperfect endings (these are very regular):**

| | -AR (*hablar*) | -ER/-IR (*comer / vivir*) |
|---|---|---|
| yo | hablaba | comía |
| tú | hablabas | comías |
| él/ella | hablaba | comía |
| nosotros | hablábamos | comíamos |
| vosotros | hablabais | comíais |
| ellos | hablaban | comían |

Only **three** verbs are irregular in the imperfect: *ser* (**era**), *ir* (**iba**), *ver* (**veía**).

steps.title: Side by side: when to use which
steps.body:
## The core contrast

Think of the imperfect as the **stage and scenery** and the preterite as the **actions of the play**.

| Use the PRETERITE for... | Use the IMPERFECT for... |
|---|---|
| A completed, single event | An ongoing action ('was -ing') |
| A chain of events ('and then...') | A habit ('used to', 'every day') |
| 'What happened' | 'What was going on' (background) |
| A sudden interruption | Description: weather, age, looks, feelings |
| A defined time span (*viví 3 años*) | Time, telling the clock (*eran las dos*) |
| *De repente, **sonó** el timbre.* | *Mientras **dormía**, todo **estaba** oscuro.* |

**Trigger words** point the way (though meaning always wins):

| Preterite cues | Imperfect cues |
|---|---|
| ayer, anoche | siempre, normalmente |
| de repente (suddenly) | mientras (while) |
| un día, una vez | todos los días (every day) |
| dos veces (twice) | mientras tanto (meanwhile) |

> **The classic combo:** imperfect sets the scene, preterite breaks it.
> *Mientras **dormía**, **sonó** el teléfono.* = While I was sleeping, the phone rang.
> The long, ongoing action (*dormía*) is imperfect; the sudden event (*sonó*) is preterite.

steps.title: Verbs that change meaning
steps.body:
## When the tense changes the meaning

A handful of common verbs mean something **different** depending on the tense. This catches almost every learner, so study these:

| Verb | Imperfect (state) | Preterite (event) |
|---|---|---|
| conocer | *conocía* = knew / was acquainted | *conocí* = **met** (for the first time) |
| saber | *sabía* = knew (a fact) | *supe* = **found out / learned** |
| querer | *quería* = wanted | *quise* = **tried / decided to** |
| no querer | *no quería* = didn't want | *no quise* = **refused** |
| poder | *podía* = was able / could | *pude* = **managed to / succeeded** |

**Examples:**

- *Ya **conocía** a Ana, pero **conocí** a su hermano ayer.* = I already knew Ana, but I **met** her brother yesterday.
- *No **sabía** la verdad; la **supe** anoche.* = I didn't know the truth; I **found out** last night.
- ***Quería** salir, pero **quise** terminar primero.* = I wanted to go out, but I **decided to** finish first.
- *No **quise** ayudarle.* = I **refused** to help him.
- ***Podía** correr rápido, y por fin **pude** ganar.* = I was able to run fast, and at last I **managed to** win.

> The pattern: the **imperfect** describes a lasting mental state; the **preterite** marks the moment something happened or changed.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish past form with its precise English meaning.
Match each trigger word with the tense it usually signals.
Translate: *While I was sleeping, the phone rang.*
exercise.hint: The long 'was sleeping' is imperfect (dormía); the sudden 'rang' is preterite (sonó).

exercise.prompt: Background description: fill in the imperfect of *hacer* (weather). 'It was sunny when we left.'
exercise.hint: Weather as background = imperfect. hacer → hacía.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the preterite of *llegar*. 'I was reading when my friend arrived.'
exercise.hint: The sudden interrupting event = preterite. llegar → llegó (él).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I met her last year.* (conocer — first meeting!)
accept: Yo la conocí el año pasado.
exercise.hint: 'met (first time)' = preterite: conocí. 'last year' = el año pasado.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I found out the truth last night.* (saber)
exercise.hint: 'found out' = preterite of saber: supe (no accent). 'last night' = anoche.

exercise.prompt: Describe an ongoing background action: which sentence means 'I was eating when you called'?
exercise.hint: 'was eating' (ongoing) = imperfect (comía); the interrupting call = preterite (llamaste).

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *When I was a child, I used to play in the park.*
exercise.hint: Both verbs are imperfect: era (ser, irregular) and jugaba (habit, -AR).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Yesterday I arrived, ate, and left.* (a sequence of finished actions)
accept: Ayer llegué, comí y me fui.
ayer llegué, comí y me fui
Ayer llegue, comi y me fui.
Ayer llegué comí y me fui.
Ayer llegué, comí y me fui
exercise.hint: Each finished step in the story = preterite: llegué, comí, me fui.
distractors: Ayer llegaba, comía y me iba.
Ayer llegué, comía y me fui.
Ayer llegaba, comí y me iba.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *She was tall and had blue eyes.* (a physical description)
accept: Era alta y tenía los ojos azules.
era alta y tenía los ojos azules
Ella era alta y tenía los ojos azules.
Era alta y tenia los ojos azules.
Era alta y tenía ojos azules.
Era alta y tenía los ojos azules
distractors: Fue alta y tuvo los ojos azules.
Era alta y tuvo los ojos azules.
Fue alta y tenía los ojos azules.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the preterite of *querer*. 'He refused to help.' (no + preterite = refused)
exercise.hint: 'refused' = no + preterite of querer: no quiso.
